fact: December 29 sits exactly 3 days before the new year, making it the perfect day for animals to start their winter naps - many bears are deep in hibernation by now in northern climates!
In the Northern Hemisphere, the sun sets super early around this time, sometimes before 5 PM, creating those amazing orange and pink winter sunsets that look like cotton candy in the sky.
This date falls in the middle of Capricorn season - people born on December 29 share their zodiac sign with mountain goats!
Evergreen trees often look extra green in late December because snow reflects additional light onto their needles, making them appear more vibrant against the white backdrop.
December 29 marks roughly 8 days after the winter solstice in the north, which means daylight is already starting to creep back - but you won't notice for weeks!
In Australia and other southern places, December 29 is peak summer - cicadas are typically buzzing so loudly they can drown out conversations!
Owls have easier hunting around this time of year in snowy regions because small animals leave tracks in the snow, making them super easy to spot from above.
Tree sap moves incredibly slowly in late December - so slowly that if you could watch it, it would be like watching paint dry in slow motion!
The moon often looks extra bright and crisp on cold December nights because there's less water vapor in the winter air to blur its light.
December 29 is prime time for seeing your breath fog up - the bigger the temperature difference between your warm lungs and the cold air, the thicker the cloud you can make!
Squirrels are frantically digging up their buried nuts right now, but they typically forget where they hid about 25% of them - those forgotten nuts will grow into new trees!
In northern regions, December 29 often brings some of the deepest snow cover of the year, creating natural igloos and snow caves that arctic foxes use as cozy hideouts.
